summary: Spolsky responds to Microsoft's 'Workplace Advantage' initiative, which reclassifies employees into four types to determine optimal workspace configurations. He acknowledges that private offices aren't ideal for every role, but firmly argues they remain essential for programmers. Drawing on his own experience with Project Aardvark interns, he describes how open spaces destroy programmer concentration by turning every two-person conversation into a group interruption. He reports that switching to more private workspaces for summer interns improved productivity without hurting communication.
key_points: Microsoft's Workplace Advantage categorizes employees into four types (travelers, orchestrators, concentrators, providers) to match workspace to work style
Private offices aren't right for every role, but remain the most productive environment for programming work
Open spaces eliminate the possibility of private conversations—every interaction becomes a group interruption that breaks flow
Formal methods like weekly status emails and informal ones like shared lunches can keep people in the loop without destroying concentration
Switching Project Aardvark interns from open space to private niches improved focus without noticeably hurting communication
money_quotes: There's not much debate that this is the most productive environment for programmers, but not everyone at Microsoft is a programmer.
Don't fall for it. They also want M&Ms for breakfast and a pony.
Open space is fun but not productive.
Every time I went out there to talk to one of them, it became a conversation with all of them.
Although this slightly helps keep everyone 'in the loop,' it also knocks programmers out of flow causing them to lose their concentration and devastating productivity.
I prefer to keep people in the loop using more formal methods, like weekly email status reports, and through informal methods like eating lunch together every day.
key_insight: Programmers who say they prefer open offices are wrong about what makes them productive—private spaces preserve flow state, and communication can be maintained through deliberate mechanisms like shared meals and status updates.
